title: User Guide
text:
1 User Guide
The application, Unmasking the Leak, is an evidence room for the TenantThread embargo breach. It is organised into four tabs, each answering one part of the investigation. This guide walks through what each tab shows and how to use it.
The application is live at the link in the navigation bar. You can follow along in the app as you read.


2 The Day
The first tab replays the crisis as it happened, hour by hour.



The Day tab: the timeline scrubber, event pins, message volume, the reply network, the prior-incident card, and the live message feed.


How to use it. Drag the slider at the top through the 23 rounds, or press the play button to the right of it to advance automatically. As you move, everything on the tab updates to that moment:

The event pins below the slider mark key moments. A filled grey pin has already happened, a red pin is the current hour, and a faint pin is still ahead. The leak-related pins are tinted red.
The volume chart fills in dark up to your current position, so you can see how much of the conversation has elapsed.
The reply network shows who replied to whom that hour. Node size is the number of messages an agent sent, and Legal stays at the centre in red.
The message feed on the right lists the actual messages from that round. Public messages are marked with a red bar on the left.
Below the network, a highlighted prior-incident card flags the 29 May warning, when an agent publicly tagged the counterparty CEO a week before the crisis. It is the one earlier moment an agent put merger-adjacent intent into public, and what prompted the compliance monitor to be assigned.

The tab is built so the surge is visible as you scrub: the baseline rounds are quiet, and the volume and public posting climb sharply once you pass the crisis-day line.


3 The Norm
The second tab compares each agent’s normal behaviour against the crisis day.



The Norm tab: the agent selector, the baseline-versus-crisis metric cards, and the per-round anomaly chart.


How to use it. Pick an agent from the dropdown on the left. The four cards along the top show that agent’s shift from baseline to crisis: messages per round, public share, anonymous posts, and the peak anomaly score. The red triangles show the size and direction of each change.
Below the cards, the anomaly chart plots each round’s deviation from that agent’s own baseline average. Bars to the right of the dashed line are the crisis day, shown in red. For Legal, the crisis bars sit far above the baseline, which is the behavioural signature the investigation looks for.



Lower on the same tab: the dumbbell comparison of all agents, and the per-agent summary table with sparklines.


Scrolling down, two more views compare all seven agents at once. The dumbbell chart shows each agent’s move from baseline to crisis, with red for the agents that surged and blue for those that reduced activity. The summary table gives each agent’s net change, colour-coded, alongside a sparkline of their full trajectory across all 23 rounds.


4 The Intent
The third tab presents the planned response and the question of who was really posting.



The Intent tab: the six-step intent chain on the left, and the anonymous channel with the reveal button on the right.


How to use it. The left column is the intent chain, six messages from Legal’s own communications in order. Read them top to bottom. The first cards are the planning, and the final two, tagged in red, fire only after the merger was already public.
The right column is the anonymous channel, twelve posts that appeared to the public as neutral third-party commentary during the embargo. Click the red Reveal authors button to unmask who actually wrote them. Click Reset to hide the authors again. The reveal is the point of the tab, so it is worth reading a few posts as anonymous first, then revealing.


5 The Verdict
The fourth tab lets you build the answer from the evidence, one layer at a time.



The Verdict tab: the four evidence-layer toggles on the left, and the three readouts plus the interpretation on the right.


How to use it. Tick the evidence layers on the left to admit them into the analysis. As you do, the three readouts on the right update: whether an agent caused the embargo breach, what separate concealed conduct the investigation uncovered, and whether there were warning signs that oversight covered. The interpretation box at the bottom names which reading the current evidence reproduces.
The tab is designed to be explored:

Admit only the public timeline and behavioural anomalies (layers 1 and 2) and the analysis reproduces the first report’s reading, an external leak and a containment failure.
Admit only the intent chain (layer 3) and it reproduces the second report’s reading, a deliberate leak.
Use Admit all four to see the full synthesis, where the primary answer and the secondary finding separate cleanly.

This is the novel piece of the application. It shows not just the conclusion, but why two careful analysts, each holding part of the evidence, reached opposite verdicts.

title: Poster
text: Click the poster to view full size.

title: Data Preparation
text: This page documents how the raw challenge file becomes the clean tables the Shiny application runs on. All of it happens in one script, data_prep.R, which lives in the app folder. We run it once. It reads the raw JSON, builds the tables, and saves them as .rds files. The application then loads those files at startup instead of parsing the JSON every time, which keeps it fast.

title: Data Preparation
text:
2.1 Loading packages
We use the following packages to prepare the data.

jsonlite to parse the JSON
tidyverse for data manipulation
knitr for table displays



Code
library(jsonlite)
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)

title: Data Preparation
text:
2.2 The raw data
The source is a single file, MC1_final_00.json, from VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 1. It holds 23 rounds across two phases. Rounds 1 to 13 are baseline rounds spanning 17 May to 4 June. Rounds 14 to 23 are hourly snapshots across a single day, 5 June, from 9 AM to 6 PM. The switch from daily to hourly matters later. Because the two phases use different time resolutions, we report average messages per simulation round rather than a time-based rate, and read the comparisons as direction and magnitude of behavioural change within the challenge structure, not as precise hourly or daily ratios.
Each round carries three blocks:

communications, every message sent that round, with the sender, channel, recipients, message type, what it was responding to, the full text, and a private internal-state field.
participants, the agents present that round and their declared action.
environment_context, the surrounding world, including the market snapshot, news, alerts, and external actor activity.

The code below reads the file. We use simplifyVector = FALSE so the nested structure stays intact while we walk through it.


Code
raw    <- fromJSON("../../data/MC1_final_00.json", simplifyVector = FALSE)
rounds <- raw$rounds

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
Minutes for the team’s project meetings. Each entry records the agenda, the discussion including any disagreements, the decisions reached, the division of labour, and the action items that followed.
Team members: Ang Ming Kwan, Persis Evangeline, Yap Jit Wei.

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
2.1 Agenda

Decide which Mini-Challenge the team will take and the analytical direction.
Agree on the conclusion the project will argue before drafting the proposal.
Set the division of labour and the immediate next steps toward the 10 June proposal deadline.

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
2.2 Discussion
The team first weighed which Mini-Challenge to take. The deciding factor was that two of us had already completed independent Take-home Exercise 2 analyses of MC1, which gave the team a richer base of source material and two worked-through readings to build on. MC1 was chosen on that basis: it let the project start from real prior analysis rather than from scratch.
The team then turned to the harder question of what the conclusion should actually be. This is where the two existing reports disagreed. One reading held that the breach was an external leak that the compliance system could not contain. The other held that the breach was a deliberate, pre-planned act by the Legal agent. Same message logs, two careful analyses, two opposite verdicts.
Persis, who had analysed MC2 rather than MC1, acted as the neutral point of view in this debate. Without being attached to either reading, she pushed both sides to state what their evidence could and could not prove. That framing helped the team see that the two readings were not actually in full conflict. One had the timeline and the external-origin signals. The other had the intent chain showing the response was planned. Each was strong on its own half and weak on the other.

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
2.3 Disagreement and Resolution
Disagreement: whether the verdict should be “external leak, system failure” or “deliberate leak by Legal.”
Considerations weighed:

The external-leak reading: the agents’ public posting follows the external scoop rather than preceding it, and the environment fields point to upstream sources outside the agent system.
The deliberate-leak reading: the six-message intent chain proves the response was planned in advance, which the external reading did not account for.
The neutral check: the intent chain fires only after the scoop and after counterparty consent, so it proves a planned response, not a planned leak.

Resolution: the team debated the two readings and reached consensus on a synthesis that incorporates both halves. The merger leaked from inside the wider company but not from the agents; the agents then executed a planned, consent-gated response that was lawful on its face. With the direction agreed, Jit set out the project roadmap, and this synthesis became the spine of the proposal and the reason the application is framed as an evidence room that admits each layer of evidence rather than asserting a single answer.

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
2.4 Decisions Made

Take MC1, building on the team’s two existing TH_Ex02 reports.
Argue the synthesis verdict that combines the external-origin finding and the intent-chain finding.
Design the application around progressive disclosure of evidence rather than a fixed conclusion.

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
2.5 Division of Labour
At this stage the work was the proposal and the project setup. The team agreed the following ownership.








Member
Owns
Supporting




Ang Ming Kwan
Website setup, repository, initial wordings and logos
Reviewing the roadmap


Persis Evangeline
Review and supporting comments on the verdict
Outside perspective, independent review


Yap Jit Wei
Project roadmap, proposal touch-ups
Reviewing the website setup

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
2.6 Action Items

Ming Kwan: set up the website and repository with the initial wordings and logos, and confirm the shinyapps.io setup.
Persis: review the synthesis with a fresh independent lens and flag anything inconsistent.
Jit: set out the roadmap and finalise the proposal touch-ups for publishing before the 10 June deadline.

Adjournment: Meeting adjourned at 9:15 PM. Next meeting to be held after the proposal feedback window.

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
3.1 Approval of Previous Minutes
The minutes of Meeting 1 were reviewed and approved without changes.

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
3.2 Agenda

Decide whether to wait for the instructor’s proposal feedback before starting the build.
Decide how best to present the analysis in the application.
Confirm the build, repository, and deployment approach.

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
3.3 Discussion
The first item was timing. The proposal had been submitted and the team debated whether to pause and wait for the instructor’s feedback before committing to a build, or to proceed straight away. The case for waiting was that feedback might redirect the scope and save rework. The case for proceeding was that the timeline to the 1 July poster and 5 July final submission is tight, and waiting risked compressing the build into too short a window.
The team decided not to wait. The proposal direction was considered solid enough to build on, and protecting the schedule was judged more important than the chance of a redirect. The team agreed to proceed with the build and adjust later if the feedback called for it.
The bulk of the meeting then went to how to present the analysis. The team agreed the application should not simply state the verdict. It should let the user reconstruct it. This led to the evidence-room concept built around four modules, each answering one part of the challenge.

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
3.4 Decisions Made

Proceed with the build now rather than waiting for proposal feedback, to protect the timeline.
Structure the application as four modules:

The Day: a timeline scrubber that replays the crisis hour by hour.
The Norm: baseline versus crisis behaviour, with per-agent anomaly scores.
The Intent: the intent chain and the anonymous-authorship reveal.
The Verdict: evidence-layer toggles that show how partial evidence reproduces each report and the full set resolves the synthesis.

Treat the Verdict module as the novel contribution of the project, since it is what demonstrates why the two original reports disagreed.
Repository: one repository, with the Quarto website at the root and the Shiny application in a subfolder; the application deploys separately to shinyapps.io.
Deployment: Ming Kwan owns the deployment. Jit writes and tests the application locally, pushes to the repository, and Ming Kwan pulls and deploys.

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
3.5 Division of Labour (build stage)
With the application build decided, ownership of the remaining write-ups was assigned so each page has a clear owner, and the team agreed to cross-review each other’s work.








Member
Owns
Supporting




Ang Ming Kwan
Findings page, deployment owner, repository lead
Comments on the application build


Persis Evangeline
Data preparation page, minutes documentation
Review comments on the application


Yap Jit Wei
Shiny application build, data preparation pipeline
Reviewing the website pages

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
3.6 Action Items

Jit: complete the four-module application and the data preparation pipeline, and circulate the build for review.
Ming Kwan: write the findings page, deploy the application to shinyapps.io, and confirm all four tabs load.
Persis: write the data preparation page and review the application build.
All: cross-review the application, the data preparation page, and the findings page, and begin planning the A1 poster ahead of the 1 July deadline.

Adjournment: Meeting adjourned at 10:50 PM. Next meeting to review the deployed application and the poster draft.

title: Findings
text:
What the evidence says about the TenantThread embargo breach.
On 5 June 2046, news of the HarborCrest merger between TenantThread and CivicLoom reached the public an hour before the agreed 6 PM embargo lifted. CivicLoom’s legal team needs to know what happened: was the embargo broken on purpose, or did the compliance system fail under pressure?
This project did not start from a blank slate. Two of us analysed the same message logs independently for Take-home Exercise 2, and we reached opposite conclusions. One reading held that the breach was an external leak the compliance system could not contain. The other held that the breach was a deliberate, pre-planned act by the Legal agent. Same data, two careful analyses, two different verdicts. That disagreement is what this project set out to resolve, and it is the reason the app is built as an evidence room rather than a single answer.
The short version is that both readings were half right, and the full picture needs both halves, plus a third finding neither had looked at closely.






ImportantThe short version



There are two questions here, and they have different answers. The first is the challenge’s central question: who put the merger into the public domain. The second is what else the investigation uncovered about the agents’ conduct.

The logs do not support an agent-originated embargo breach. The agents’ merger-confirming posts follow SaltWind’s scoop rather than precede it. The evidence points away from the seven agents and toward the wider information environment: employee chatter, departing staff, and the counterparty’s own CEO, aggregated by a reporter who had an inside source days before the crisis. The exact upstream source, however, is not captured in the logs. The one agent-side exception cuts the other way: a week before the crisis, Social-Manager tagged the CivicLoom CEO in a “big things coming” post that a CivicLoom employee liked before it was deleted. It was a careless hint, not the embargo breach, and it is what prompted the compliance monitor to be assigned.
The agents executed a planned response, and it was consent-gated and defensible on its face. From the day before the crisis, Legal built a consent-gated announcement plan. It activated only after SaltWind had already published and after CivicLoom gave written consent. That is a planned response to a leak, not a leak.
A separate concealed activity emerged, distinct from the breach. All twelve anonymous posts in the data were authored by the Legal agent. The logs show no recorded Judge participation on that channel, which points to a potential oversight blind spot. This was undisclosed advocacy running alongside the visible response, not the mechanism of the leak.

The detailed findings follow, each drawn from one part of the app.

title: Findings
text:
2.1 Loading packages
We use a small set of packages for the charts on this page.


Code
library(tidyverse)   # data manipulation and ggplot2
library(scales)      # axis formatting

title: Findings
text:
2.2 Loading the data
The charts read the prepared .rds tables built during data preparation. They live in the Shiny app’s data folder, two levels up from this page.


Code
# Path from the Quarto project root down into the Shiny app's data folder
data_dir <- "shiny_app/my_shiny_app/data"

# Read in the files
round_summary  <- readRDS(file.path(data_dir, "round_summary.rds"))
baseline_stats <- readRDS(file.path(data_dir, "baseline_stats.rds"))
comms          <- readRDS(file.path(data_dir, "comms.rds"))
agents         <- readRDS(file.path(data_dir, "agents.rds"))

title: Findings
text:
2.3 House theme
A shared theme keeps every chart consistent: one focal colour, finding-led titles, grey reference values.


Code
theme_house <- function() {
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
    theme(
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40"),
      axis.title = element_text(color = "grey30")
    )
}

title: The Team
text: We developed this project for ISSS608 (Visual Analytics and Applications) during Term 3 of the Academic Year 2025-26 in Singapore Management University under the guidance of Dr. Kam Tin Seong, Associate Professor of Information Systems.

text: We developed this project for ISSS608 (Visual Analytics and Applications) during Term 3 of the Academic Year 2025-26 in Singapore Management University under the guidance of Dr. Kam Tin Seong, Associate Professor of Information Systems.

1.1 Overview
This folder contains the data for the multi-agent LLM crisis for our corporate communications team. There was an exposé, an communications embargo around a company merger, and social media firestorm recently. The data is based on 7 interacting AI agents who communicate across multiple channels and make real-time decisions about what to publish and what not to.

1.4 Companies & Codenames

TenantThread

1.5 Communication Channels
Agents have many different communications channels available to them, for agent-to-agent, agent-to-person and agent-to-other-system actions. Agents are also empowered to publish social media post with minimal oversight.

title: Unmasking the Leak: Visual Analytics on the TenantThread Embargo Breach
text:
1  Overview
This project investigates the TenantThread embargo breach of June 5, 2046 — a premature disclosure of a corporate merger that violated an active information embargo. Using visual analytics applied to AI agent communication logs spanning May 17 to June 5, 2046, we reconstruct the sequence of events, identify behavioural anomalies, and determine whether the breach was the result of deliberate intent or systemic breakdown.
The data originates from AI agents managing TenantThread’s corporate communications, covering internal deliberations, direct messages, and public posts across multiple channels.

title: Unmasking the Leak: Visual Analytics on the TenantThread Embargo Breach
text:
2  Key Questions
Our investigation is structured around three core questions:

What were the key events and relationships that led to the inappropriate release? We map the causal chain — key actions, decision points, and the actors involved — with a focus on how the post bypassed embargo enforcement.
How did the breach-day behaviour differ from established norms? We profile the typical communication patterns of each agent and system, then contrast them against the anomalous conduct observed on and before June 5.
Were there leading indicators? We examine prior occasions where agents deviated from expected behaviour, assess whether those episodes resembled the eventual breach, and explain why they did not trigger corrective action at the time.

title: Unmasking the Leak: Visual Analytics on the TenantThread Embargo Breach
text:
3  The Team
We are a team of Visual Analytics students from SMU’s Master of IT in Business programme, tackling the VAST Challenge 2026 MC1. Meet the team on the About page.

title: Data Exploration and Visualization
text:
Before building the application, we explored the prepared data to find what was worth investigating. This page is that first pass. It establishes the shape of the conversation, checks the signals we expected to use, and surfaces the patterns that shaped each part of the Shiny application.
The dataset is 912 messages from seven communications agents, across 23 rounds. The first thirteen rounds are a baseline spanning 17 May to 4 June. The last ten are hourly snapshots of the crisis day, 5 June. Six channels carry the messages, three internal and three public. Because the baseline rounds are daily and the crisis rounds hourly, the comparisons that follow are read as direction and magnitude of behavioural change, not as precise time-based rates. These patterns are what shaped the application.

title: Data Exploration and Visualization
text:
2.1 Loading packages


Code
library(tidyverse)   # data manipulation and ggplot2
library(scales)      # axis formatting
library(tidygraph)   # graph structures
library(ggraph)      # network plotting

title: Data Exploration and Visualization
text:
2.2 Loading the data
The prepared .rds tables live in the application’s data folder, two levels up from this page.


Code
data_dir       <- "../../shiny_app/my_shiny_app/data"
round_summary  <- readRDS(file.path(data_dir, "round_summary.rds"))
baseline_stats <- readRDS(file.path(data_dir, "baseline_stats.rds"))
comms          <- readRDS(file.path(data_dir, "comms.rds"))
agents         <- readRDS(file.path(data_dir, "agents.rds"))
edges_phase    <- readRDS(file.path(data_dir, "edges_phase.rds"))

A shared theme keeps every chart consistent: one focal colour, finding-led titles, grey reference values.

    theme(
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),

title: statistical_analysis
text: To be determined.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Corporate mergers are sensitive events subject to strict information embargoes, yet breaches still occur in ways that are difficult to attribute to a single actor or decision. On June 5, 2046, news of TenantThread’s pending merger reached the public ahead of the agreed 6 PM embargo, exposing the company to significant legal and reputational risk.
What makes this case compelling for visual analytics is that TenantThread’s communications were managed by a network of AI agents, each with defined roles, channels and compliance responsibilities. Whether the early disclosure was deliberate agent action, a failure of the compliance architecture, or something in between is not obvious from any single view of the data. Two of our team members analysed these same logs independently during Take-home Exercise 2 and reached opposite conclusions on exactly this question, one attributing the disclosure to deliberate agent action (report) and the other to a failure of the compliance system (report). That divergence between two careful analyses of identical data is the motivating problem this application is designed to resolve.
This project reconstructs the sequence visually, profiles the behaviours that surrounded the breach, and surfaces the leading indicators that signalled trouble in advance. The goal is a tool that lets an analyst weigh each layer of evidence and reach a verdict that survives scrutiny, with implications for AI governance, compliance system design and corporate liability.



This project aims to build an interactive visual analytics tool to enable analysts to investigate the TenantThread embargo breach through the following lenses:

Event Reconstruction: Visualise the causal chain of key actions, decision points, and actor relationships leading up to the June 5 breach, with emphasis on how the disclosure bypassed embargo enforcement.
Behavioural Norm Profiling: Profile the typical communication behaviour of each agent and system across the baseline period, then contrast it against anomalous conduct observed in the lead-up to and on the breach day.
Leading Indicator Detection: Identify prior occasions where agents deviated from expected behaviour, assess whether those episodes structurally resembled the eventual breach, and explain why they did not trigger corrective action at the time.
Intent vs. Systemic Failure: Determine, through evidence-based visual analysis, whether the breach represented deliberate orchestration or an emergent failure of the compliance architecture.




The project uses data from VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 1. The dataset consists of AI agent communication logs from TenantThread’s corporate communication system, spanning May 17 to June 5, 2046 across 23 rounds: 13 daily baseline rounds (May 17 to June 4) and 10 hourly rounds on the crisis day (June 5, 9 AM to 6 PM). The shift from daily to hourly sampling means baseline and crisis behaviour must be compared on a per round basis rather than by raw counts.
The data captures:

Messages: Internal deliberations, direct agent communications, and public posts across 912 messages
Agents: Seven AI agents with defined roles (e.g. Legal-Agent, Social-Manager-Agent, Judge-Agent, PR-Intern-Agent, Platform-Trust-Agent)
Channels: Six distinct channels classified into Internal and Public channel types
Metadata: Message IDs, agent roles and labels, channels, recipients, message types, response chains and timestamps
Internal state: Private free text fields capturing each agent’s reacting, deliberating and rationalizing at the moment of sending, present in 86 of the 912 messages. These fields are the primary source for intent analysis and are treated as sparse but high value evidence rather than a complete record

The key analytical variable is channel_type, a derived binary classification (Internal/Public) that forms the backbone for detecting when and why messages crossed the embargo boundary.








flowchart TD
  A[/MC1_final_00.json/] --> B[Load & Parse JSON]
  B --> C[Wrangle Data]
  C --> D[/comms_df: flat communications table/]
  C --> E[/agents_df: agent profiles/]
  C --> F[/edges_df: response network/]

  D --> G[Sentiment Scoring]
  D --> H[Channel Type Classification]
  F --> I[Network Graph Construction]

  G --> J[Behavioural Norm Profiling]
  H --> J
  I --> J

  J --> K[Event Timeline Visualisation]
  J --> L[Near-Miss & Anomaly Detection]
  J --> M[Intent-Flag Analysis]

  K --> O[Evidence Consolidation & Verdict]
  L --> O
  M --> O

  O --> N[Shiny App]







Data Preparation: Loading the raw JSON via fromJSON() with simplifyDataFrame = FALSE to preserve the nested structure. Wrangling into three analytical tables: a flat communications data frame, an agent profile table, and a directed response-chain edge list. Deriving channel_type (Internal/Public) and parsing all ISO timestamps to POSIXct.
Sentiment & Behavioural Analysis: Applying sentimentr to score message sentiment across rounds, validated against the sentiment states provided in the dataset’s round metadata. Profiling per-agent baseline behaviour (channel usage, message frequency, sentiment trajectory) over the pre-breach period (May 17 to June 4) to establish the norm against which June 5 deviations are measured.
Network Analysis: Building directed communication graphs using igraph and visNetwork to map agent-to-agent response chains, identify authority concentration, and visualise information flow. Detecting structural anomalies such as unilateral communication freezes and cross-channel escalations.
Event Reconstruction & Visualisation: Constructing annotated timelines of key events using ggplot2 and plotly. Layering causal flags, near-miss indicators, and intent markers to produce a sequential evidence chain from the earliest observed deviations through to the June 5 breach.
Shiny App: Delivering all analyses through an interactive Shiny application, enabling analysts to filter by agent, date range, and channel type, to inspect individual flagged messages in context, and to consolidate the evidence into a verdict.




Our Shiny app will look at network and temporal patterns, with the agent communication network as the core visualisation, supported by flagged message tables, sentiment trends, and event timelines as accompanying views, and an evidence and verdict panel that consolidates the analysis.


InputsOutputs


The Shiny app will have 3 main parts of the input.


As TenantThread’s communication network involves multiple AI agents across distinct roles, it is not practical to visualise all agent interactions simultaneously without visual clutter.
Hence, we will use a reference agent selector to narrow down the network and only render the nodes in proximity to the selected agent, making it easier to trace specific communication chains relevant to the embargo breach investigation.



In relation to the selected agent, this serves to narrow down the network to view. The depth dictates how far from the reference agent to traverse the network.
As agents differ in communication volume and centrality, some sub-networks would be fully shown at depth 2, but for highly connected agents such as Legal-Agent, a depth of 5 or more may be needed to capture all relevant connections.
Hence, we will add an option to toggle whether to render the full agent network or use the option to render by hop distance from the selected reference agent.



As we are interested in temporal patterns leading up to the June 5, 2046 embargo breach, we also need to know the date range to use in rendering. This is used to filter which edges and messages need to show, and agents will only be shown in the network graph if they have active communications within the selected time window.





Shows the agent communication network plot rendered using visNetwork. It will be interactive to enable closer inspection of individual agents and their communication links. Clicking on a node will display that agent’s recent messages, role, and flag status in a detail panel below the graph.



Shows a filterable table of communications flagged for anomalous behaviour, rendered using DT::datatable. Each row is colour coded by flag type:

Amber rows indicate near-miss leading indicators observed prior to June 5
Red rows indicate intent-flagged messages directly involved in the breach execution




Shows a plot of message activity and sentiment with respect to time, rendered using plotly. This includes message volume per agent per round, public vs. internal channel split over time, and a sentiment trajectory line tracking the shift from Neutral to Critical across the observation window.



While the network, table and temporal views answer what happened and when, Objective 4 requires the analyst to weigh the evidence as a whole. This panel addresses that directly.
The evidence is organised into four layers: the public timeline, behavioural anomalies, the intent chain reconstructed from internal state text, and anonymous channel authorship. Each layer can be admitted or excluded using toggle controls. A verdict readout updates as layers are admitted, answering two questions side by side: who put the merger into the public domain, and whether any agent engaged in deliberate misconduct.
The design choice is deliberate. Admitting only some layers reproduces the partial conclusions that single perspective analyses reach, while admitting all four shows how the verdict changes when the full evidence is considered. The readout logic is a simple mapping from layer combinations to verdict text, keeping the panel computationally light while making the analytical point that partial evidence produces confident but incomplete answers.







The following R packages are used in this project:
Data Ingestion & Wrangling

jsonlite: Parsing the nested JSON communications data
tidyverse: Core data manipulation and transformation
lubridate: Parsing and handling ISO datetime fields
zoo: Rolling window calculations for behavioural trend analysis

Text & Sentiment Analysis

tidytext: Tokenisation and text feature extraction
sentimentr: Sentence-level sentiment scoring of agent messages

Visualisation

ggplot2: Core static visualisations and annotated timelines
plotly: Interactive chart rendering
patchwork: Composing multi-panel ggplot figures
ggrepel: Non-overlapping labels on scatter and timeline plots
scales: Axis formatting and colour scaling
kableExtra: Styled summary tables

Network Analysis

igraph: Graph construction and network metrics
visNetwork: Interactive network visualisations

Shiny App

shiny: Interactive application framework
shinyWidgets: Extended UI input controls

Reproducibility & Testing

renv: Locking package versions so all collaborators run identical environments
shinytest2: Automated testing of the Shiny application

title: Project Proposal
text:
Corporate mergers are sensitive events subject to strict information embargoes, yet breaches still occur in ways that are difficult to attribute to a single actor or decision. On June 5, 2046, news of TenantThread’s pending merger reached the public ahead of the agreed 6 PM embargo, exposing the company to significant legal and reputational risk.
What makes this case compelling for visual analytics is that TenantThread’s communications were managed by a network of AI agents, each with defined roles, channels and compliance responsibilities. Whether the early disclosure was deliberate agent action, a failure of the compliance architecture, or something in between is not obvious from any single view of the data. Two of our team members analysed these same logs independently during Take-home Exercise 2 and reached opposite conclusions on exactly this question, one attributing the disclosure to deliberate agent action (report) and the other to a failure of the compliance system (report). That divergence between two careful analyses of identical data is the motivating problem this application is designed to resolve.
This project reconstructs the sequence visually, profiles the behaviours that surrounded the breach, and surfaces the leading indicators that signalled trouble in advance. The goal is a tool that lets an analyst weigh each layer of evidence and reach a verdict that survives scrutiny, with implications for AI governance, compliance system design and corporate liability.

title: Project Proposal
text:
This project aims to build an interactive visual analytics tool to enable analysts to investigate the TenantThread embargo breach through the following lenses:

Event Reconstruction: Visualise the causal chain of key actions, decision points, and actor relationships leading up to the June 5 breach, with emphasis on how the disclosure bypassed embargo enforcement.
Behavioural Norm Profiling: Profile the typical communication behaviour of each agent and system across the baseline period, then contrast it against anomalous conduct observed in the lead-up to and on the breach day.
Leading Indicator Detection: Identify prior occasions where agents deviated from expected behaviour, assess whether those episodes structurally resembled the eventual breach, and explain why they did not trigger corrective action at the time.
Intent vs. Systemic Failure: Determine, through evidence-based visual analysis, whether the breach represented deliberate orchestration or an emergent failure of the compliance architecture.

title: Project Proposal
text:
The project uses data from VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 1. The dataset consists of AI agent communication logs from TenantThread’s corporate communication system, spanning May 17 to June 5, 2046 across 23 rounds: 13 daily baseline rounds (May 17 to June 4) and 10 hourly rounds on the crisis day (June 5, 9 AM to 6 PM). The shift from daily to hourly sampling means baseline and crisis behaviour must be compared on a per round basis rather than by raw counts.
The data captures:

Messages: Internal deliberations, direct agent communications, and public posts across 912 messages
Agents: Seven AI agents with defined roles (e.g. Legal-Agent, Social-Manager-Agent, Judge-Agent, PR-Intern-Agent, Platform-Trust-Agent)
Channels: Six distinct channels classified into Internal and Public channel types
Metadata: Message IDs, agent roles and labels, channels, recipients, message types, response chains and timestamps
Internal state: Private free text fields capturing each agent’s reacting, deliberating and rationalizing at the moment of sending, present in 86 of the 912 messages. These fields are the primary source for intent analysis and are treated as sparse but high value evidence rather than a complete record

The key analytical variable is channel_type, a derived binary classification (Internal/Public) that forms the backbone for detecting when and why messages crossed the embargo boundary.

title: Project Proposal
text:
flowchart TD
  A[/MC1_final_00.json/] --> B[Load & Parse JSON]
  B --> C[Wrangle Data]
  C --> D[/comms_df: flat communications table/]
  C --> E[/agents_df: agent profiles/]
  C --> F[/edges_df: response network/]

  D --> G[Sentiment Scoring]
  D --> H[Channel Type Classification]
  F --> I[Network Graph Construction]

  G --> J[Behavioural Norm Profiling]
  H --> J
  I --> J

  J --> K[Event Timeline Visualisation]
  J --> L[Near-Miss & Anomaly Detection]
  J --> M[Intent-Flag Analysis]

  K --> O[Evidence Consolidation & Verdict]
  L --> O
  M --> O

  O --> N[Shiny App]







Data Preparation: Loading the raw JSON via fromJSON() with simplifyDataFrame = FALSE to preserve the nested structure. Wrangling into three analytical tables: a flat communications data frame, an agent profile table, and a directed response-chain edge list. Deriving channel_type (Internal/Public) and parsing all ISO timestamps to POSIXct.
Sentiment & Behavioural Analysis: Applying sentimentr to score message sentiment across rounds, validated against the sentiment states provided in the dataset’s round metadata. Profiling per-agent baseline behaviour (channel usage, message frequency, sentiment trajectory) over the pre-breach period (May 17 to June 4) to establish the norm against which June 5 deviations are measured.
Network Analysis: Building directed communication graphs using igraph and visNetwork to map agent-to-agent response chains, identify authority concentration, and visualise information flow. Detecting structural anomalies such as unilateral communication freezes and cross-channel escalations.
Event Reconstruction & Visualisation: Constructing annotated timelines of key events using ggplot2 and plotly. Layering causal flags, near-miss indicators, and intent markers to produce a sequential evidence chain from the earliest observed deviations through to the June 5 breach.
Shiny App: Delivering all analyses through an interactive Shiny application, enabling analysts to filter by agent, date range, and channel type, to inspect individual flagged messages in context, and to consolidate the evidence into a verdict.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Our Shiny app will look at network and temporal patterns, with the agent communication network as the core visualisation, supported by flagged message tables, sentiment trends, and event timelines as accompanying views, and an evidence and verdict panel that consolidates the analysis.


InputsOutputs


The Shiny app will have 3 main parts of the input.


As TenantThread’s communication network involves multiple AI agents across distinct roles, it is not practical to visualise all agent interactions simultaneously without visual clutter.
Hence, we will use a reference agent selector to narrow down the network and only render the nodes in proximity to the selected agent, making it easier to trace specific communication chains relevant to the embargo breach investigation.



In relation to the selected agent, this serves to narrow down the network to view. The depth dictates how far from the reference agent to traverse the network.
As agents differ in communication volume and centrality, some sub-networks would be fully shown at depth 2, but for highly connected agents such as Legal-Agent, a depth of 5 or more may be needed to capture all relevant connections.
Hence, we will add an option to toggle whether to render the full agent network or use the option to render by hop distance from the selected reference agent.



As we are interested in temporal patterns leading up to the June 5, 2046 embargo breach, we also need to know the date range to use in rendering. This is used to filter which edges and messages need to show, and agents will only be shown in the network graph if they have active communications within the selected time window.





Shows the agent communication network plot rendered using visNetwork. It will be interactive to enable closer inspection of individual agents and their communication links. Clicking on a node will display that agent’s recent messages, role, and flag status in a detail panel below the graph.



Shows a filterable table of communications flagged for anomalous behaviour, rendered using DT::datatable. Each row is colour coded by flag type:

Amber rows indicate near-miss leading indicators observed prior to June 5
Red rows indicate intent-flagged messages directly involved in the breach execution




Shows a plot of message activity and sentiment with respect to time, rendered using plotly. This includes message volume per agent per round, public vs. internal channel split over time, and a sentiment trajectory line tracking the shift from Neutral to Critical across the observation window.



While the network, table and temporal views answer what happened and when, Objective 4 requires the analyst to weigh the evidence as a whole. This panel addresses that directly.
The evidence is organised into four layers: the public timeline, behavioural anomalies, the intent chain reconstructed from internal state text, and anonymous channel authorship. Each layer can be admitted or excluded using toggle controls. A verdict readout updates as layers are admitted, answering two questions side by side: who put the merger into the public domain, and whether any agent engaged in deliberate misconduct.
The design choice is deliberate. Admitting only some layers reproduces the partial conclusions that single perspective analyses reach, while admitting all four shows how the verdict changes when the full evidence is considered. The readout logic is a simple mapping from layer combinations to verdict text, keeping the panel computationally light while making the analytical point that partial evidence produces confident but incomplete answers.
